other_observers: Brandon Percival for most of the time Mark Peterson for about 10 minutes Brad Stager for 20 seconds; Brandon Percival found the bird in his yard earlier in the afternoon
specific_location: Near the intersection, but uphill on Harmony, of Harmony Drive and Acorn Drive., within sight of Brandon Percival's house on Harmony.
description: This is a dark buteo-like (but not) hawk with a long tail, upright posture, with reddish wing patches. The cere was boldly yellow on a hooked beak. The face was dark chestnut but there was a faint supercillium above the eye and there appeared to be a light eyelid surrounding the dark iris. The back of the head and nape was also dark chestnut brown but with light verticle streaks in it. The upper breast, seen on the perched bird was dark with wide white verticle streaks, but the light streaks were less wide than the surrounding dark "streaks". This was visible on the perched bird. The long yellow legs were visible along with yellowish talons.   In flight the bird had three areas of white underneath. The first was large white patches or windows on the inner primaries larger in size as compared to a Rough-legged Hawk. A second area of white was the lower belly and undertail coverts. It too was sizable and contrasted strongly with the birds upper belly breast (which didn't look as streaked in flight, face and much of the bird's tail However the tail appeared more like that of an adult showing as being dark for three quaters of its length with a small terminal white tip and a wide white bar at the proximal end of the tail.with a wide white subterminal band. At rest, the long tail was very frayed at the distal end and the coloration as described above was not noticeable on its tree perch. At rest the legs were noticabley longer than any North American Buteo and unfeathered tarsi.   On the perched bird the rufous areas were confined to the shoulder, but was not too visible on the angle I had but was much more visible when the bird flew. The rufous leg feathers were barred with thin white bands. The other area of rufous was just above the white lower belly and undertail, but this could actually have been the long leg feathers hanging down.   The posture of the perched bird was significant in that it showed a hunched back at times, but when alert before moving, it had an upright posture.Behaviors: I watched the bird for over an hour where it was partially hidden from view while perched in the top center of a large tree. It hopped out of view twice and once it held its wings half closed and fell straight down and out of view. The bird was later seen flying low in a direct flight for about 200 meters where it landed on a roof top for about five minutes before flying out of sight, for me. It then was flushed by two birders, one who hadn't seen it yet and the bird ended up in the darkening skies, perched in another large tree, about 1/4 mile away from where I had first see it. The one time the bird disappeared (found again perched lower in the same tree) it is possible that it was hunting something on the trunk or lower branches of that tree.Call: none heardPlumage: in its 1st year apparently molting
photographer: Photographer: bill maynard, bill maynard, bill maynard
